nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded forest pools; shallow water over soft sandy or muddy substrate with heavy leaf litter, submerged roots and driftwood; typically in dense riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: caves or coconut huts for spawning
catappa/Indian almond leaves for tannins and pH buffering
dense planting with hardy low-light species (Java fern, Anubias)
dim lighting to mimic blackwater conditions

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and tiny crustaceans. In the aquarium, offer small live and frozen foods: baby brine shrimp, micro worms, daphnia, cyclops, and small bloodworms. High-quality micro-pellets and frozen foods are generally accepted once acclimated, but variety is important. Feed small amounts multiple times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, cardinal tetras)
corydoras (small species, e.g. C. pygmaeus, C. hastatus)
blackwater bottom dwellers that avoid territory
avoidWith: Apistogramma baenschi (Inca Apisto) — hybrid risk and interspecific aggression
other Apistogramma males in small tanks
dwarf shrimp (fry and shrimp predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Apistogramma nijsseni; male in breeding coloration. Verify availability before use.
Wikimedia Commons candidate; check for current availability and photographer attribution on the file page.

summary: A striking Peruvian blackwater dwarf cichlid named for ichthyologist Han Nijssen. Females bear a bold 3-spot 'panda' pattern; males develop vivid blue-green and yellow nuptial colors. Strict blackwater requirements (pH 4.5–6.0) make this an advanced species. Do not mix with A. baenschi due to hybrid risk.
careNotes:
Apistogramma nijsseni is the type species of the A. nijsseni species group and is endemic to blackwater streams and flooded forests in the Ucayali drainage of Peru. It is nicknamed the 'Panda Apisto' or 'Panda Dwarf Cichlid' because females display a distinctive three-spot black pattern on their yellow body — a caudal spot, a lateral spot, and a cheek spot — reminiscent of a giant panda's markings. Males are considerably larger and develop vivid blue-green iridescent scales, red-orange dorsal and caudal fin coloration, and elongated dorsal fin rays during breeding condition.

This is a strict blackwater species and one of the more demanding Apistogramma in terms of water chemistry. pH must be maintained in the 4.5–6.0 range for long-term health and successful breeding; neutral or alkaline water has been reported to cause rapid decline, immune suppression, and reproductive failure. Use reverse osmosis or rainwater remineralized to low hardness (gH 1–6, kH 0–3). Peat filtration, catappa/Indian almond leaves, and driftwood all help maintain the acidic, tannin-rich conditions. Dark substrate and subdued lighting reduce stress.

Like other Apistogramma, A. nijsseni is haremic in the wild — one male holds a territory containing several female territories, each with a spawning cave. In the aquarium a single male with two or three females in a 75–100 L heavily decorated tank is commonly recommended. Multiple caves and line-of-sight breaks allow females to hold their own micro-territories without constant male harassment. The male may be separated after spawning if the female shows excessive aggression; females are aggressive brood guarders and will turn intensely yellow-orange when guarding eggs or wrigglers.

Important compatibility note: A. nijsseni is frequently confused with and hybridized alongside A. baenschi (Inca Apisto, fw-fish-194) in the trade, as both are Peruvian, similar in size, and share overlapping pH tolerances. These two species must not be kept together — hybridization readily occurs and contaminates both lines. Inspect purchase stock carefully and source from reputable specialist breeders.

Feed multiple small portions daily. Live and frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, micro worms) are strongly preferred, especially to condition fish for breeding. High-quality micro-pellets may be accepted over time. Avoid large tankmates; best kept with small, peaceful blackwater community fish that occupy different tank zones.
breedingNotes:
Cave spawner; females deposit eggs on the ceiling or inner walls of a cave, coconut hut, or similar shelter. Typical clutch sizes are reported at 30–80 eggs. The female guards and fans the eggs; larvae hatch in approximately 48–72 hours and become free-swimming after a further 4–6 days. Females in breeding color turn a vivid chrome-yellow and will aggressively defend the spawning territory against all tankmates including the male — separating the male post-spawning is advisable in smaller tanks.

Strict blackwater conditions (pH 4.5–5.5, very soft water, tannin-rich) are generally necessary to reliably trigger spawning and achieve good egg fertility. Conditioning with live foods prior to attempting breeding is strongly recommended. Fry can be raised on infusoria and newly hatched baby brine shrimp, progressing to micro-pellets as they grow. Breeding is rated difficult due to the stringent water chemistry requirements, the sensitivity of eggs and fry to water quality fluctuations, and the need for specialist live foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma nijsseni
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided water parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, dH 0–6, temperature 24–28°C), adult size (male to 7 cm SL, female to 4 cm SL), native range (Peru, Ucayali drainage), habitat (blackwater streams and pools), diet (micropredator), cave spawning behavior, female brood care, and haremic social structure. Species group classification (nijsseni-group type species) confirmed here.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma nijsseni
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes, Cichlidae), native range (Peru), adult size (max 7.5 cm TL males), water parameters, and distribution. Confirmed species described by Kullander 1979; type locality Rio Pisqui, Ucayali system, Peru.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Apistogramma care guide; cross-checked tank size recommendations, grouping ratios (1 male: 2–3 females), cave requirements, diet (live/frozen small invertebrates), and compatibility notes. nijsseni-specific water chemistry requirements verified against SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma nijsseni profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked female panda coloration pattern description, advanced care rating, hybrid risk with A. baenschi, and blackwater biotope specifics. Confirmed 'Panda Apisto' common name usage in trade.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Apistogramma baenschi (hybrid risk cross-reference)
additional.notes: Consulted to confirm hybrid risk between A. nijsseni and A. baenschi (fw-fish-194, Inca Apisto). Both are Peruvian blackwater Apistogramma of the nijsseni group; they hybridize readily in captivity. Separation and avoidWith entry added accordingly.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner with strong female brood care. Strict blackwater conditions (pH 4.5–5.5, very soft water) required for reliable spawning. Conditioning with live foods essential. Females guard eggs aggressively; male separation in smaller tanks often necessary.
